speaker: patient
text: Hi, Doctor. I've had a pretty bad headache since this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: I see. When did the headache start, and is it constant or intermittent? Any nausea or visual changes?

speaker: patient
text: It started around 7 a.m. today — sharp, throbbing, mostly behind my right eye. No nausea, but I’ve felt unusually thirsty all day.

speaker: doctor
text: Let me check your blood glucose — that’s likely relevant given your diabetes history. Hmm, it’s 13.7 mmol/L. That’s significantly elevated.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is also up at 112 bpm — tachycardic for resting state. Let’s take your temperature and blood pressure next.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve been feeling hot and shaky too, and I urinated more than usual this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: That fits with hyperglycemia. Did you take your insulin as scheduled this morning?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on today’s readings and your symptoms, I’m continuing your Insulin glargine at 16 units nightly — but we’ll need to monitor closely.

speaker: doctor
text: Your temperature is 39.6 °C — that’s a high fever. This isn’t just hyperglycemia; there’s likely an acute infection triggering this decompensation.

speaker: doctor
text: Your diastolic blood pressure is 86 mmHg — borderline low given your tachycardia and fever. We need to rule out sepsis or UTI.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve had some burning when I pee since yesterday — I thought it was just dehydration.

speaker: doctor
text: That’s important — urinary symptoms plus fever and hyperglycemia raise concern for pyelonephritis. Let’s get a urinalysis and blood cultures stat.

speaker: patient
text: My pulse feels really fast now — like it’s pounding in my ears.

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — your heart rate has climbed further to 130 bpm, which is concerning in the context of rising fever and possible infection. We’ll start IV fluids and empiric antibiotics immediately.

speaker: patient
text: Should I still take my evening insulin?

speaker: doctor
text: Yes — but I’ll adjust the dose based on hourly glucose checks. We may add rapid-acting insulin for correction if needed.

speaker: patient
text: The chills came back about an hour ago — and my head hurts even worse now.

speaker: doctor
text: We’ll give acetaminophen for the fever and headache, but the priority is treating the underlying infection. Are you able to keep fluids down?

speaker: patient
text: Not really — I vomited once after drinking water.

speaker: patient
text: That fever hasn’t broken — it’s still 39.6 °C, just like the reading you took earlier.
